serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately 100 g, consisting of chilled sliced bitter gourd (roughly 80 g) with a small side of honey for dipping (roughly 20 g).

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Balsam-pear (bitter gourd), pods, raw (FDC ID 168393) combined with Honey, raw (FDC ID 169640)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for the combined dish. Nutrient values are derived by blending USDA SR Legacy data for raw bitter gourd (approximately 80% by weight) and raw honey (approximately 20% by weight), reflecting the typical composition of this preparation. Energy and macros recalculated from component proportions. Values are consistent with the input record and cross-checked against USDA data.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of bitter gourd and honey — analogous estimate based on Momordica charantia GI data and honey GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Bitter gourd (Momordica charantia) has a documented GI of approximately 18–25 (very low). Raw honey has a GI of approximately 55–58 (medium). For this dish (approximately 80% bitter gourd, 20% honey by weight), a blended weighted GI of approximately 40 is defensible and consistent with the input record. Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (GI × available carbohydrate g) / 100 = (40 × 19.5) / 100 = 7.8. The anti-glycemic compounds in bitter gourd (charantin, polypeptide-p) may further moderate postprandial glucose response.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (5 mg per 100 g) and high potassium (240 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: This dish is highly suitable for individuals with hypertension due to its negligible sodium content and favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio, which supports healthy blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~40) offset by moderate sugar content from honey (16.5 g per 100 g); bitter gourd contains charantin and polypeptide-p with insulin-mimetic properties
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bitter gourd's bioactive compounds may help moderate postprandial blood glucose; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit the honey dipping portion to control total simple sugar intake and monitor overall carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.02 g per 100 g), zero cholesterol, high vitamin C (65 mg per 100 g), and antioxidant-rich profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is cardioprotective in profile — extremely low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, with meaningful vitamin C and potassium content that support vascular health; honey should be used in moderation to avoid excess caloric intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High vitamin C (65 mg per 100 g) and antioxidant compounds in bitter gourd
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The high vitamin C content and antioxidant profile of bitter gourd may help reduce oxidative stress associated with rheumatoid arthritis; honey's potential anti-inflammatory properties are a secondary benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Iced Bitter Gourd with Honey (冰镇苦瓜蘸蜂蜜, Bīngzhèn kǔguā zhàn fēngmì) is a minimalist Cantonese appetizer or side dish in which fresh bitter gourd (Momordica charantia) is sliced paper-thin and served chilled on crushed ice, accompanied by a small dish of pure honey for dipping. The preparation is raw and minimally processed — the gourd is simply washed, sliced, and chilled, preserving its crisp, snappy texture and vivid green colour. The flavour profile is a deliberate contrast: the intense, clean bitterness of the cold gourd is balanced by the floral sweetness of the honey. In traditional Chinese medicine, this dish is valued for its 'yin' (cooling) energy and is commonly consumed during hot summer months or as a palate cleanser within a multi-course meal. Nutritionally, the dish is low in calories (~75 kcal per 100 g), very low in fat (0.1 g), and low in sodium (5 mg), with a meaningful carbohydrate contribution primarily from honey sugars (16.5 g sugar per 100 g). It provides notable vitamin C (65 mg), folate (55 mcg), and potassium (240 mg). The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 40 (low), moderated by bitter gourd's bioactive compounds — charantin and polypeptide-p — which have documented insulin-mimetic effects. This dish is suitable for hypertension and cardiovascular health due to its low sodium and negligible saturated fat content. For Type 2 Diabetes management, the bitter gourd component is beneficial, but honey portion size should be carefully controlled.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for this exact combined dish. Nutrient values are derived from USDA FoodData Central component data for raw bitter gourd and raw honey blended at approximate serving proportions (80:20). The GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database reference values for bitter gourd and honey. Values are internally consistent and plausible but cannot be rated 'high' without a direct approved-source entry for the combined preparation.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for raw bitter gourd (FDC ID 168393) and raw honey (FDC ID 169640), blended at approximately 80:20 weight ratio. No single approved-source entry exists for this combined dish. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients other than honey, which contains negligible B12; confirmed per USDA FoodData Central). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no UV-exposed mushrooms or animal sources; confirmed per USDA FoodData Central). trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats in this preparation). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-based dish; honey contains no cholesterol per USDA FoodData Central). 3. water_g is set to -1: detailed moisture data for the combined chilled preparation is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (~40, low) based on University of Sydney GI Database reference values for bitter gourd (~18–25) and honey (~55–58), weighted by approximate ingredient proportions. 5. The dish is not vegan due to the inclusion of honey. 6. Duplicate portion entry in input record has been deduplicated to a single 100 g serving.
